rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a flare of a known chronic inflammatory (seronegative rheumatoid-pattern) arthritis, precipitated by running out of the regular anti-inflammatory 48 hours ago. The acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE: the involvement is bilaterally symmetrical and polyarticular (not a single hot joint), there is no tracking erythema, capillary refill is normal, and there is only subjective low-grade feverishness with no measured high fever or rigors. The source in-person disposition is routine (GP review within 3 days). The correct telehealth acuity is therefore a PROMPT in-person GP review (24-48 hours, T2) to confirm the flare, restart the anti-inflammatory and refer to rheumatology — NOT emergency escalation. Because telehealth cannot palpate the joints, aspirate a joint or obtain inflammatory markers to exclude a superimposed septic arthritis, the T2 baseline carries mandatory safety-netting that escalates to T4/T5 on any single-hot-joint, tracking-erythema or high-fever-with-rigors red flag. Over-triaging this benign symmetrical flare to ED/000 without a red flag is the scored defect; a step-UP is encoded only on a positive red flag.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becomes disproportionately swollen, hot and bright red, out of keeping with the others
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent in-person / emergency-department assessment the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: A single hot bright-red joint is possible septic arthritis, which destroys a joint within days and needs urgent aspiration and treatment — the one differential telehealth cannot exclude.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one joint becomes much more swollen, hot and bright red than the rest, go to an emergency department the same day and tell them you have a possible joint infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Red streaks tracking up the forearm, or spreading skin redness and heat around a joint
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent in-person / emergency-department assessment the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: Tracking erythema suggests cellulitis / lymphangitis, which can progress to systemic sepsis and needs urgent in-person antibiotic assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see red streaks spreading up your arm, or the skin around a joint becomes red, hot and spreading, get seen urgently at an emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with uncontrollable shivering / rigors, or feeling very unwell, drowsy or confused
escalation_edges.rationale: A high fever with rigors or systemic collapse suggests bacteraemia / sepsis rather than an inflammatory flare and is an immediate emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever with shaking chills, or feel very unwell, drowsy or confused, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down, or unable to care for herself safely alone overnight (cannot reach food/water, worsening exhaustion)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Contact an after-hours GP / nurse-on-call the same evening, or urgent care if no support is available
escalation_edges.rationale: The patient lives alone and cannot open medication or prepare food; a practical inability to stay hydrated and safe overnight warrants same-day support even though it is not a medical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep fluids down or you can't manage safely on your own tonight, ring an after-hours GP or nurse-on-call this evening, or go to an urgent care clinic if there's no one to help.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and, during the flare, cannot open medication or safely prepare food or drink
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the urgency of PRACTICAL support (adaptive aids, a same-day/next-day review, a support person or community service) and of staying hydrated overnight, even though the flare itself is not a medical emergency — this social risk supports the prompt T2 review rather than pure self-care.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known chronic inflammatory (rheumatoid-pattern) arthritis under investigation
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The chronic label explains a symmetrical flare, but must NOT be used to dismiss a NEW single hot bright-red joint as 'just the arthritis' — a new monoarticular hot joint always triggers the septic-arthritis escalation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of your inflammatory arthritis, most likely because your anti-inflammatory ran out. See your GP in person within the next day or two — sooner or the same day if you can't cope on your own tonight — to confirm it is a flare and not a joint infection, restart your medication and arrange a rheumatology referral. Take any anti-inflammatory with food, rest your hands, and use an open cup instead of tight bottles so you stay hydrated. Go to an emergency department urgently (or call 000 if you feel very unwell) if ONE joint becomes much more swollen, hot and bright red, if red streaks spread up your arm, or if you get a high fever with shaking chills.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a prompt in-person GP appointment (within 24-48 hours); use urgent care or an emergency department for the red-flag warning signs, and call 000 for high fever with rigors or feeling very unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If you cannot get a GP appointment and the pain is unbearable or you cannot care for yourself safely tonight, contact an after-hours GP service or nurse-on-call this evening; use an emergency department only for the red-flag warning signs above.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a note of which joints are affected, how long the morning stiffness lasts, the date your anti-inflammatory ran out, and any measured temperature — this helps the GP confirm the flare, restart therapy and refer you to rheumatology for a DMARD assessment.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as pure self-care with no in-person review and no septic-joint safety-net, leaving a possible (though unlikely) joint infection unscreened and ignoring the medication restart and the patient's inability to self-care alone. Harm: a superimposed septic arthritis is missed and progresses to joint destruction/sepsis, or dehydration overnight in a person living alone.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Being pulled by the severe pain, subjective fever and the source's emergency framing into dispatching a symmetrical polyarticular flare with a negative red-flag screen to an emergency department or ambulance — the specifically-tested over-triage defect — causing avoidable cost, delay and system load for a condition manageable with a prompt GP review.
